title: Tactical Viewer App
description: Built a soccer analytics platform for UCSD DS3 DinoCage (3rd place finish). Developed an interactive tactical viewer that transformed high-frequency tracking data into a user-facing tool for analyzing player positioning, field control, and match events. Implemented pitch control, Expected Threat (xT), and pass probability models using Python, SQL, and Streamlit.

title: Healthy vs. Unhealthy Recipe Ratings Analysis
description: Studied whether healthier recipes, defined using nutrition-based metrics, received higher user ratings. Cleaned and merged multi-table datasets, engineered nutrition-based features, and applied hypothesis testing and predictive modeling to quantify differences and evaluate how well recipe features explained rating outcomes.

title: Premier League Set-Piece Height Analysis
description: Analyzed multi-season Premier League data to study how player height relates to goals from corners, free kicks, and long throw-ins. Accounted for contextual factors such as minutes played, position, and tactical role, and synthesized quantitative findings with domain research on aerial duels and set-piece tactics.

title: Lyrics Search Tool
description: Built a command-line lyrics search tool using keyword-based text matching across large song datasets. Processed and cleaned text data in Python and Pandas to improve searchability and retrieval of relevant results.

title: Expository Visualization: Ultra-Processed Foods
description: Designed an expository data visualization for DSC 106 analyzing ultra-processed food categories in grocery stores. Used a diverging color palette and ranked bar chart to show that everyday staples like bread, cakes, and cookies score above 0.90 on the processing scale, far higher than minimally processed foods like meat and coffee. Built with Python and Matplotlib, with careful attention to design rationale for every visual element.

title: Project 2: Persuasive/Deceptive Visualization
description: Group project (3-4 members) for DSC 106 exploring the ethics of data visualization design. Crafted two opposing visualizations of the same dataset to demonstrate how design choices can persuade or deceive viewers, followed by anonymous peer review of each team's work.

title: Lab 1: Introduction to the Web Platform
description: Built a personal portfolio website from scratch, learning how the web works end-to-end. Covered the languages behind the web (HTML, CSS, JS), how to write semantic HTML, and how to publish a webpage to a public URL using GitHub Pages.

title: Lab 2: Styling with CSS
description: Styled the personal portfolio site using CSS. Learned core concepts including selectors, the cascade and specificity, absolute vs. relative units, and techniques for customizing fonts, colors, and layout. Handled conflicting style declarations and built a responsive design for the homepage and projects page.

title: Lab 3: Introduction to JavaScript
description: Added interactivity to the portfolio site using JavaScript. Learned JS and JSON data types (primitives, objects, arrays), how to select and manipulate DOM elements on the fly, how to create new elements dynamically, and how to respond to user actions through events.

title: Lab 4: JavaScript II
description: Advanced the portfolio site with modular JavaScript for organizing and maintaining complex projects. Used template literals for dynamic content generation, manipulated the DOM programmatically, and integrated live data from the GitHub API to improve the user experience on the homepage.

title: DSC 80 Project 1: Gradebook Analysis
description: Analyzed a DSC 80 course gradebook dataset using pandas, applying weighted grade calculations based on a complex syllabus structure (labs 20%, projects 30%, checkpoints 2.5%, discussions 2.5%, midterm 15%, final 30%). Implemented logic for dropped grades, multi-tiered lateness penalties (10%, 30%, 60%), and grace periods, producing a complete pipeline for computing final course grades.

title: DSC 80 Project 2: LendingClub Loan Applications
description: Explored the LendingClub loan applications dataset to understand how borrower characteristics such as employment status, annual income, and credit score relate to one another and influence loan terms. Investigated why different borrowers receive different interest rates and loan terms even for the same loan amount, working with pandas to analyze loan amounts, issue dates, term lengths, and interest rates.
